turns.content: I'm struggling to organize my crate like I would with a Go package, where everything lives in one directory. Can you explain how the Rust module system handles this differently?

Think of it like organizing a library: instead of putting every book on one shelf, you create specific sections for different genres. In Rust, the root directory acts as your main collection, and each subdirectory becomes a separate module. When you define a file like mod.rs or bar.rs inside that structure, you're creating distinct namespaces. The compiler treats these files as individual modules unless you explicitly tell it otherwise using pub use statements.
So if I have a main module and several sub-modules like utils or config, do they automatically become part of the library root?

Not exactly. By default, each file defines its own private namespace unless you use pub use to re-export them from the parent module. If your library root is lib.rs and you have a sub-module called utils.rs, that utility code stays hidden unless you explicitly declare it with pub use utils; in your main crate definition.

Why does this even work this way? I have a big crate and it feels like I'm just dumping everything into one file, but then my build breaks because of module resolution errors. Can't you just put the logic in bar.rs and use mod.rs to hide it?

It works that way because Rust's compiler needs a specific entry point to resolve your crate boundaries, which is why the library root must define what constitutes the public interface. When you separate code into files like bar.rs and re-export it with pub use, you are telling the compiler exactly which modules should be visible outside your crate while keeping internals hidden. This separation helps manage dependencies so that when you update bar.rs, the main entry point doesn't break unexpectedly.
But wait, if I just move the logic to bar.rs and use mod.rs for re-exports, why does my CI still fail with unresolved types? Isn't that all I need?

The issue is likely that your library root isn't actually exporting the module you want to expose as part of the public API. Even if bar.rs exists and uses pub use correctly, if mod.rs doesn't declare it in its visibility list or if there are missing dependencies in the dependency graph, the compiler won't recognize those types at runtime. The solution is ensuring your library root explicitly lists every re-exported module so that downstream crates can find them without needing to search through internal files.
